entities.description: An electronic text of Heuser's complete 1903 article: German preface, Middle English poem, and German Bemerkungen. It represents the printed edition rather than manuscript images.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00082
notes: The item page labels Heuser as author, but for the cataloged medieval poem he is editor, transcriber, and commentator rather than author.
The item contents explicitly expose preface, poem, and Bemerkungen divisions.

bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, 'Eine neue mittelenglische Version der Theophilus-Sage,' Englische Studien 32 (1903): 1-23.
bibliographic.title: Eine neue mittelenglische Version der Theophilus-Sage

entities.description: The first publication of the Rawlinson tail-rhyme version. German introduction occupies printed pp. 1-5, the manuscript rubric and complete 107-stanza poem pp. 5-23, and the Bemerkungen close p. 23.
entities.label: Wilhelm Heuser, Eine neue mittelenglische Version der Theophilus-Sage (1903)
notes: Heuser identifies MS. Rawl. poet. 225 as a badly mutilated paper manuscript whose fragments were frequently remounted on paper.
He rejects Rutebeuf's play as the direct exemplar and leaves the version's transmission unresolved.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1903, and both U-M and the Google scan identify the underlying book as public domain. Heuser died in 1924, but no worldwide claim is needed here.
rights.component: 1903 article text, edition, commentary, and typography

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Special Collections mediated copying order form submitted by email or post
access.contact: mediated.copying@bodleian.ox.ac.uk; Reader Services, Weston Library, Bodleian Libraries, Broad Street, Oxford OX1 3BG, UK
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225
access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans with shelfmark and folio metadata

localCopies.label: Bodleian Imaging Services terms of use

notes: Available to all, subject to size, condition, legal restrictions, and scanning capacity; some material cannot be scanned.
Checked price: £1 per scan for non-Oxford users, with a £10 minimum; average turnaround about 10 working days.

For a complete source request, specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every manuscript leaf; separately identify ff. 11-15 as the work locus. Heuser's description of mutilation makes conservation approval especially material.

rights.basis: The current mediated-copying form says scans of out-of-copyright material are © Bodleian Libraries and supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0; current terms allow specified non-commercial and academic uses and require permission for commercial uses.
rights.component: Reference scans of out-of-copyright material supplied through Bodleian mediated copying
notes: The license is noncommercial and does not authorize for-profit print-on-demand reuse.
Confirm the license and permitted repository use in the item-specific delivery correspondence.

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Imaging Services order form submitted by email or post
access.contact: Imaging Services, Bodleian Libraries, Broad Street, Oxford OX1 3BG, UK; specialcollections.enquiries@bodleian.ox.ac.uk for manuscript enquiries
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225

localCopies.label: Bodleian image order terms and conditions

notes: Checked order-form prices: £27 for the first image, £7.50 for subsequent images 2-99, £6.15 for subsequent images 100+, and a £20 minimum. Large or difficult orders are quoted separately and prepaid.
Standard turnaround is 20 working days from payment, subject to retrieval, fragility, security, and handling; the requested manuscript's mutilated condition may prevent or limit imaging.
Request the complete physical object and separately map ff. 11-15; do not describe a target-folio order alone as a complete manuscript facsimile.

rights.basis: The current order forms say scans of out-of-copyright material are © Bodleian Libraries and supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0; current terms allow specified non-commercial and academic uses and require permission for commercial uses.
rights.component: High-resolution images of out-of-copyright material supplied by Bodleian Imaging Services
notes: The license is noncommercial and does not authorize for-profit print-on-demand reuse.
A separately linked older Image Order Terms PDF says all reproduction requires written permission; this unresolved conflict is recorded below.

rights.basis: The separately linked Image Order Terms and Conditions state that Bodleian retains copyright in all reproductions and that customers may not reproduce supplied material for any reason without written permission. This conflicts with the current forms and Terms of Use.
rights.component: Images supplied under the linked Image Order Terms and Conditions
notes: Obtain item-specific written clarification before redistributing ordered images in the Git repository or using them commercially.

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Archives and Manuscripts Requests account and Weston Library Level 1 reading room
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225
access.format: Onsite consultation of the physical manuscript, subject to access and conservation approval

entities.description: The sole known witness of this tail-rhyme Theophilus version, at ff. 11-15, in an imperfect South English Legendary and saints' lives miscellany. Heuser reports a mutilated paper codex with fragments remounted on paper.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225
notes: DIMEV reports LALME/eLALME Linguistic Profile 73, grid 548 300, localized to Ely.
DIMEV locates the Latin incipit rubric on f. 11 and 'Explicit Tyofle' on f. 15.

physicalDescription.extent: Target work at ff. 11-15; the official catalog does not state a total leaf count
notes: Official catalog: an imperfect South English Legendary with additional saints' lives, some unique to this manuscript.
Heuser: strongly mutilated, with fragments and scraps frequently pasted onto new paper; exact current conservation structure awaits image or curatorial verification.

rights.basis: The anonymous text is preserved in a 1450-1500 witness and predates modern copyright terms by centuries. This does not grant rights in modern photographs, editions, transcriptions, or provider interfaces.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English text
rights.jurisdiction: United States, United Kingdom, and ordinary life-plus-70 jurisdictions

rights.basis: The current mediated-copying form says scans of out-of-copyright material are © Bodleian Libraries and supplied under CC BY-NC 4.0; current terms allow specified non-commercial and academic uses and require permission for commercial uses.

bibliographic.citation: Beverly Boyd, ed., The Middle English Miracles of the Virgin (New York: Columbia University Press, 1955), 68-87.
bibliographic.title: The Middle English Miracles of the Virgin

entities.description: A later complete edition of the Rawlinson poem on pp. 68-87, as identified by DIMEV. The present audit did not establish whether Boyd transcribed the manuscript directly or revised Heuser.
entities.label: Beverly Boyd, The Middle English Miracles of the Virgin (1955)

bibliographic.citation: Beverly Boyd, ed., The Middle English Miracles of the Virgin (San Marino, CA: Huntington Library, 1964).
bibliographic.title: The Middle English Miracles of the Virgin

entities.description: The Huntington Library issue of Boyd's edition. DIMEV groups it with the 1955 Columbia publication; the accessible Internet Archive metadata identifies a 1964 San Marino edition.
entities.label: Beverly Boyd, The Middle English Miracles of the Virgin (Huntington Library, 1964)

rights.basis: The 1955/1964 scholarly edition is modern and Internet Archive restricts the scan to controlled digital lending; no open license or United States renewal-status determination was verified.
rights.component: Boyd's modern edition, introduction, notes, and typography
rights.jurisdiction: United States and other applicable jurisdictions
notes: The medieval poem itself remains public domain; do not copy Boyd's editorial matter without a separate basis.

DIMEV places the complete Rawlinson poem edition at pp. 68-87.

rights.basis: The 1955/1964 scholarly edition is modern and Internet Archive restricts the scan to controlled digital lending; no open license or United States renewal-status determination was verified.

entities.label: Internet Archive controlled-lending scan of Boyd's 1964 edition

bibliographic.citation: Beverly Boyd, 'The Rawlinson Version of Theophilus,' Modern Language Notes 71.8 (1956): 556-559.
bibliographic.title: The Rawlinson Version of Theophilus

entities.description: A four-page critical note with short extracts, not a complete edition. Boyd distinguishes three Middle English versions and argues that tail-rhyme romance conventions better explain this version's dialogue and narrative handling than a direct miracle-play source.
entities.label: Beverly Boyd, The Rawlinson Version of Theophilus (1956)

rights.basis: The article is modern scholarship, and the public Internet Archive issue supplies no item-level open license. No renewal or other copyright-status determination was completed in this audit.
rights.component: 1956 critical article and extracts
rights.jurisdiction: United States and other applicable jurisdictions

entities.description: The current versioned work record for IMEV/NIMEV 1883, with unique-witness locus, manuscript titles, form, rhyme pattern, subjects, linguistic localization, and bibliography.
entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse record 3109 (v1.0)
notes: Record 3109's descNote points to records 10 and 5061 as other versions. Record 5061 is actually an unrelated advice poem in this release; record 5554 is the South English Legendary Theophilus and reciprocally points to 3109.

rights.basis: The release README and Zenodo metadata apply Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.
rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data files and documentation

next_steps: Ask specialcollections.enquiries@bodleian.ox.ac.uk whether an institutional, preservation, or legacy surrogate exists and whether a complete source can be supplied.
If images become public, cache the entire published manuscript object and map ff. 11-15 rather than downloading only target leaves.
openQuestions.question: Does Bodleian hold or plan a complete digital surrogate of MS. Rawl. poet. 225 outside the currently searchable Digital Bodleian catalog?

next_steps: Request item-specific written clarification before publishing ordered facsimiles in the Git repository or using them in any commercial or print-on-demand workflow.
Retain the exact supplied invoice, license, and correspondence as access-specific evidence if an order is fulfilled.
openQuestions.question: Which current Bodleian terms control reuse of newly ordered images when the order forms and Terms of Use permit CC BY-NC or enumerated academic reuse but the separately linked Image Order Terms require written permission for every reproduction?

next_steps: Ask U-M whether production metadata identifies an original 1903 issue or a later photographic reprint; keep the encoding linked to the 1903 article text rather than to either cached scan until then.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy of Englische Studien 32 was used to create the U-M encoding?

next_steps: Inspect Boyd's introduction and textual note through lawful controlled-lending or a library copy before adding a transcribes, collated_against, or version_of edge to the poem edition.
openQuestions.question: Did Boyd's 1955/1964 complete edition transcribe MS. Rawl. poet. 225 directly, revise Heuser, or combine both?

next_steps: Inspect a legible image of f. 11 or request a curatorial reading; preserve both sourced forms in descriptive metadata until then.
openQuestions.question: Does the manuscript rubric on f. 11 read 'liberatus' as Heuser prints or 'deliberatus' as DIMEV records?

next_steps: Report the internal release-data discrepancy to DIMEV and avoid treating unrelated record 5061 as a Theophilus version.
openQuestions.question: Should DIMEV record 3109's pointer to record 5061 be corrected to record 5554?

next_steps: Review later comparative scholarship; do not create an upstream edge to Rutebeuf, the Northern Homily Cycle, or the South English Legendary without direct dependency evidence.
openQuestions.question: What earlier textual source, if any, directly underlies this unique tail-rhyme version?

reviewNotes: The abstract medieval poem, Heuser's article, original journal volume, 1965 reprint, physical manuscript, later scholarship, DIMEV record, U-M encoding, and checked-in XML are distinct layers.
The poem is the cataloged work even though CME00082 retains Heuser's complete German article framing.
A cached complete printed volume is not a manuscript facsimile; no local manuscript images are present.
Local research caching does not by itself grant permission to redistribute scans or use them in commercial print-on-demand products.

lineage.summary: CME00082 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic transcription of Wilhelm Heuser's complete 1903 article, including its German introduction and comments and its edition of the anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme Theophilus poem. Heuser transcribed the unique copy in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225, ff. 11-15. A complete Google scan of the original Englische Studien 32 and a complete scan of a 1965 Johnson photographic reprint are cached and mapped to the article. Beverly Boyd's 1956 reassessment and the complete DIMEV v1.0 release are also cached. DIMEV identifies Boyd's 1955/1964 book as a later complete edition; its 1964 scan is available only through controlled digital lending. No public manuscript facsimile was found in the live Digital Bodleian catalog on 2026-07-12, so the manifest records separate whole-codex mediated-copying and professional-imaging routes and the exact ff. 11-15 work locus.

catalogingSubject.label: The Legend of Theophilus (Rawlinson tail-rhyme version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The single anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme version of the Theophilus legend preserved at ff. 11-15 of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225 and represented by CME00082. It is not the complete manuscript, the other Middle English Theophilus versions, Wilhelm Heuser's complete 1903 German article, either scanned carrier volume, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Rawlinson tail-rhyme Theophilus poem
notes: Heuser's German preface and Bemerkungen are retained in CME00082 but are edition framing, not child parts of the medieval work.
contentParts.summary: The complete 107-stanza Middle English legend with one embedded Latin acclamation near the close.

assertion.notes: No normalized composition range is supplied because the witness date is not automatically the work's date.

dateStatements.display: Not securely established; the sole witness is from 1450-1500, while Heuser suggests that the poem may preserve an older original

assertion.notes: The date belongs to the sole surviving witness and is not a normalized composition date.

dateStatements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century, in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225

assertion.notes: Heuser presents the previously unknown manuscript text, Boyd calls him the first editor, and DIMEV lists no earlier edition.

extent.description: One complete verse legend. The poem division contains 107 numbered LG elements and 643 L elements. DIMEV's intrinsic description is 107 six-line aabccb stanzas; the encoding count is one line higher than 107 times six because LG 106 includes the separate Latin acclamation 'Te deum laudamus'. The six additional L elements elsewhere in the XML are editorial comparison quotations in Heuser's preface.

formStatements.description: A tail-rhyme poem of 107 normally six-line stanzas with rhyme pattern aabccb; stanza 106 also quotes the one-line Latin acclamation 'Te deum laudamus'.

assertion.notes: Boyd argues that the poem's dialogue and narrative conventions are better explained by tail-rhyme romance than by a direct miracle-play source.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German introduction and commentary, French comparison lines, and the Latin witness rubric are edition or paratext layers and are not whole-work languages.

scope.description: The quoted acclamation 'Te deum laudamus' encoded as the seventh L element in stanza group 106

notes: The preferred English title is supplied for discovery and disambiguation; the manuscript's Latin rubric and explicit are retained separately.
The unique Rawlinson poem must not be conflated with CME00065's Northern Homily Cycle Theophilus or DIMEV 5554's South English Legendary version.
German and French in CME00082 belong to Heuser's edition layer. Latin is included as a work language only for the embedded 'Te deum laudamus' acclamation, not for the surrounding rubric alone.
The anti-Jewish stereotype subject note supports responsible discovery and contextualization of harmful narrative content.
Manuscript image availability, whole-object request routes, local caches, and reuse terms are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review post-Boyd scholarship on the Rawlinson version's language, meter, and relationship to older English and Continental Theophilus traditions without converting witness date into composition date.
openQuestions.question: Can the composition date be narrowed independently of the 1450-1500 date of the sole witness?

next_steps: Review modern comparative scholarship; retain Heuser's rejection of Rutebeuf as a direct exemplar and Boyd's genre-based explanation as separate interpretations until a direct textual dependency is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: What earlier textual tradition, if any, directly underlies the Rawlinson tail-rhyme version?

next_steps: Inspect a legible image of f. 11 or request confirmation from Bodleian Special Collections; preserve both sourced readings until then.
openQuestions.question: Does the manuscript rubric read 'liberatus' as Heuser prints or 'deliberatus' as DIMEV records?

next_steps: Report the release-data discrepancy to DIMEV: record 5061 is an unrelated advice poem, while record 5554 is the South English Legendary Theophilus version and reciprocally points to record 3109.
openQuestions.question: Should DIMEV record 3109's related-version pointer to record 5061 be corrected to record 5554?

assertion.notes: DIMEV reports LALME/eLALME Linguistic Profile 73, grid 548 300, localized to Ely. This is a localization of scribal language, not proof that the poem was composed there.

Heuser's complete transcription of the poem from MS. Rawl. poet. 225, ff. 11-15
German introduction and Bemerkungen retained around the poem in CME00082 but excluded from the abstract medieval work

subjects.label: Clerical ambition and a pact with the devil
Anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype
notes: The tale portrays an unnamed Jewish intermediary as the agent who leads Theophilus to Satan. This heading records harmful anti-Jewish narrative content rather than endorsing it.

summaries.value: Theophilus, an archdeacon proud of his wealth and rank, suddenly loses his prosperity. An unnamed Jewish intermediary offers to restore him and leads him to Satan, whom Theophilus serves after renouncing Christ and Mary and signing a charter in his own blood. His rank and wealth restored, Theophilus later hears the heavenly command 'Revertere', repents, and appeals to the Virgin. Mary descends to hell, overcomes Satan, retrieves the charter, and returns it to Theophilus, who closes with praise of her miracles. The poem reshapes a Marian miracle as a compact tail-rhyme narrative and contains a harmful anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype. CME00082 retains Heuser's German introduction and editorial comments around the complete Middle English poem, but those modern frames are not part of the cataloged medieval work.

The poem division has 107 LG elements and 643 L elements. DIMEV describes 107 six-line stanzas; the extra encoded line is the Latin quotation 'Te deum laudamus' inside LG 106. A raw count of all 649 XML L elements also includes six French or Middle English comparison lines in Heuser's German preface, so it is not the poem's line count.

DIMEV identifies only MS. Rawl. poet. 225, ff. 11-15, for this version and distinguishes it from the Northern Homily Cycle and South English Legendary versions. Heuser rejects Rutebeuf's play as a direct exemplar and leaves the poem's more precise source relationship unresolved; Boyd instead explains its treatment through tail-rhyme romance conventions.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, following the established Theophilus identification and Beverly Boyd's Rawlinson-version title while distinguishing this text from the Northern Homily Cycle and South English Legendary versions
titles.value: The Legend of Theophilus (Rawlinson tail-rhyme version)

notes: Heuser's transcription begins 'Listenyth bothe, grete & smale, / I wil ȝow tellen a litel tale'.
titles.value: Listeneth both great and small / I will you tellen a little tale

assertion.notes: The manuscript image is not public, and Heuser prints 'liberatus' rather than DIMEV's 'deliberatus'.

work.summary: Theophilus, an archdeacon proud of his wealth and rank, suddenly loses his prosperity. An unnamed Jewish intermediary offers to restore him and leads him to Satan, whom Theophilus serves after renouncing Christ and Mary and signing a charter in his own blood. His rank and wealth restored, Theophilus later hears the heavenly command 'Revertere', repents, and appeals to the Virgin. Mary descends to hell, overcomes Satan, retrieves the charter, and returns it to Theophilus, who closes with praise of her miracles. The poem reshapes a Marian miracle as a compact tail-rhyme narrative and contains a harmful anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype. CME00082 retains Heuser's German introduction and editorial comments around the complete Middle English poem, but those modern frames are not part of the cataloged medieval work.
